disease.name: Thyroid Cancer
disease.note: Most common endocrine malignancy. Papillary thyroid cancer (PTC) accounts for 80% and has excellent prognosis. In Kenya, late presentation with large neck masses and lymph node involvement is common. Medullary thyroid cancer may be familial (MEN2). Anaplastic thyroid cancer is rare but rapidly fatal. Total thyroidectomy with central neck dissection is the standard for most, with radioactive iodine for high-risk differentiated cancers. Calcitonin screening for MTC should be considered in all thyroid nodules.
presentingComplaints: Neck lump that has been growing
Lump in the neck that moves when swallowing

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Metastatic/anaplastic/airway compromise/extensive nodal disease -> SEVERE; differentiated early-stage (T1-T2, N0) -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: When did you first notice the neck lump?
Has the lump been growing? How fast?
Have you noticed any lumps in your neck (swollen glands)?
Has your voice changed or become hoarse?
Do you have difficulty swallowing foods?
Do you have difficulty breathing, especially lying flat?
Do you have any pain in the neck or throat?
Have you lost weight unintentionally?
Do you have any family history of thyroid cancer or goiter?
Is there any family history of MEN2 (thyroid cancer, pheochromocytoma, hyperparathyroidism)?
Have you had any radiation to the neck or chest area in the past?
Were you exposed to radiation as a child (Chernobyl, radiotherapy)?
Do you come from an area with known iodine deficiency?
Have you had any previous thyroid surgery or biopsy?
Do you have a history of Hashimoto's thyroiditis?
Have you noticed any skin changes or lumps elsewhere?
Do you have any bone pain, cough, or shortness of breath?
Are you experiencing palpitations, heat intolerance, or tremors?
Have you seen a traditional healer or used herbal treatments for this lump?
How long did you wait before coming to hospital?

examination_findings.finding: Palpable thyroid mass — firm to hard in consistency
Mass fixed to underlying tissues or mobile with swallowing
Cervical lymphadenopathy — firm, fixed, enlarged nodes
Vocal cord immobility on laryngoscopy (RLN invasion)
Mass >4cm or extends beyond thyroid capsule
Horner's syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhydrosis) — sympathetic chain invasion
Signs of hyperthyroidism if functioning metastases (rare)
Hepatomegaly or lung findings (if distant metastases)
Bone tenderness or pathological fracture (if bone metastases)

bedside_scores.name: ATA Risk Stratification System for Differentiated Thyroid Cancer

components.name: Low risk: intrathyroidal PTC, <4cm, no nodal mets, no invasion
Intermediate risk: ETE, >4cm, central nodal mets, vascular invasion
High risk: gross ETE, incomplete resection, lateral nodal mets, distant mets

bedside_scores.interpretation: Low risk: lobectomy alone sufficient; Intermediate: total thyroidectomy + RAI if indicated; High: total thyroidectomy + central neck dissection + RAI + TSH suppression <0.1

subtypes.name: Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma (PTC)
subtypes.management: Total thyroidectomy if bilateral, nodal mets, >4cm, or high-risk features. Thyroid lobectomy alone if unifocal <4cm, intraglandular, no nodal mets, no ETE. Central neck dissection (level VI) if cN+ or cN0 high-risk. Radioactive iodine (RAI) ablation for high/intermediate-risk post-op. TSH suppression therapy. Surveillance with thyroglobulin + US.

subtypes.name: Follicular Thyroid Carcinoma (FTC)
subtypes.management: Total thyroidectomy. RAI ablation for all except minimally invasive FTC. TSH suppression. Surveillance with thyroglobulin and imaging. Distant metastases: RAI, TKIs for RAI-refractory.

subtypes.name: Medullary Thyroid Carcinoma (MTC)
subtypes.management: Total thyroidectomy + bilateral central (level VI) and lateral neck dissection (levels II-V). Pre-op screening for pheochromocytoma (MEN2) before surgery. Genetic testing for RET mutation. No RAI. TKIs (Vandetanib, Cabozantinib) for metastatic MTC. Surveillance with calcitonin and CEA.

subtypes.name: Anaplastic Thyroid Carcinoma (ATC)
subtypes.management: Multimodal: debulking surgery (if resectable), external beam radiotherapy (EBRT), chemotherapy (Taxol + Carboplatin or Doxorubicin). Tracheostomy for airway obstruction. Molecular testing: Dabrafenib + Trametinib (if BRAF V600E). Best supportive care. Urgent MDT discussion.

bedside.name: Vocal cord assessment (laryngoscopy/nasendoscopy)
bedside.indication: Pre-op RLN function — document baseline, detect palsy

bedside.name: Neck examination with swallowing
bedside.indication: Assess mobility, nodule consistency, lymph node survey

laboratory.name: TSH, free T4, free T3
laboratory.indication: Thyroid function — TSH level affects FNA interpretation on cytology

laboratory.name: Thyroglobulin (Tg)
laboratory.indication: Tumor marker for PTC/FTC — pre-op baseline; undetectable post-op = cure

laboratory.name: Anti-Tg antibodies
laboratory.indication: Interferes with Tg measurement; must be measured with Tg

laboratory.name: Calcitonin
laboratory.indication: Screening for MTC — elevated level is highly specific

laboratory.name: CEA
laboratory.indication: Elevated in MTC — useful for monitoring

laboratory.name: RET mutation testing (blood)
laboratory.indication: If MTC suspected — confirm hereditary nature, screen family

laboratory.name: BRAF V600E testing (on FNA)
laboratory.indication: Prognostic marker for PTC, guides extent of surgery

imaging.name: Thyroid Ultrasound (high-resolution) + Color Doppler
imaging.indication: Gold standard for nodule characterization and lymph node assessment

imaging.name: FNA with Bethesda System classification
imaging.indication: Definitive cytological diagnosis — Bethesda V/VI = surgical

imaging.name: CT Neck with IV contrast
imaging.indication: Assess extent of disease, lymph node involvement, and tracheal compression

imaging.name: CT Chest
imaging.indication: Lung metastases — especially if advanced or aggressive histology

imaging.name: FDG-PET/CT
imaging.indication: For metastatic disease, RAI-refractory disease, rising Tg with negative RAI scan

imaging.name: MRI Brain
imaging.indication: If symptoms of brain metastases or advanced disease

management.protocolReference: ATA Guidelines for Thyroid Cancer Management (2015, updated 2024)

resuscitation: For airway compromise from large/anoplastic tumor: head-up position, oxygen, IV steroids (Dexamethasone 8-16mg), urgent ENT/airway assessment, possible tracheostomy
conservative: For low-risk PTC (<1cm, intraglandular, no nodal mets): active surveillance (microPTC) may be an option
Patient counseling on surgical options: lobectomy vs total thyroidectomy, risks (RLN, hypoparathyroidism)

surgical: Total thyroidectomy + central neck dissection (level VI): for PTC >4cm, bilateral, ETE, nodal mets, high-risk histology
Thyroid lobectomy: for low-risk PTC (unifocal <4cm, intraglandular, cN0)
Total thyroidectomy: for FTC, MTC, and all high-risk PTC
Lateral neck dissection (levels II-V): for lateral neck node metastases confirmed on FNA
En bloc resection: for ATC if resectable and no distant mets
nonOperative: Radioactive iodine (RAI-131) ablation: for high/intermediate-risk differentiated thyroid cancer after total thyroidectomy
TKIs (Sorafenib, Lenvatinib) for RAI-refractory metastatic disease
Dabrafenib + Trametinib for BRAF V600E mutant ATC
Radiotherapy for ATC or R1 margins

immediate: Monitor airway, voice (RLN function), and calcium (hypoparathyroidism screen)
Calcium monitoring: iCa or corrected calcium at 6h, 12h, 24h, then daily
day3_7: Histology results: size, margins, ETE, nodal status, molecular markers
ATA risk stratification for RAI decision
day7_14: TSH check at 6-8 weeks for dose adjustment
If RAI planned: low-iodine diet education, stop thyroxine (or use rhTSH injections)

pathways.criteria: T1-T2, N0/Nx, no ETE, unifocal, no aggressive histology, >18 years
pathways.treatment: Thyroid lobectomy alone (if <4cm and intraglandular). Total thyroidectomy if >4cm. No RAI. TSH suppression target 0.5-2.0 mU/L. Surveillance with Tg and US.

pathways.criteria: T3, N1a, minimal ETE, vascular invasion, multifocal, BRAF V600E positive
pathways.treatment: Total thyroidectomy + central neck dissection. RAI ablation. TSH suppression target 0.1-0.5 mU/L. Tg + US surveillance every 6-12 months.

pathways.criteria: T4, N1b, distant metastases, gross ETE, incomplete resection, MTC, ATC
pathways.treatment: Total thyroidectomy + lateral neck dissection. RAI for high-risk DTC. TKIs for MTC and RAI-refractory DTC. Multimodal therapy for ATC. Palliative care for advanced ATC.

operative_templates.procedure: Total Thyroidectomy + Central Neck Dissection (Level VI)
operative_templates.indication: Thyroid cancer (PTC >4cm, bilateral, ETE, nodal mets; FTC; MTC; high-risk PTC)
operative_templates.approach: Cervical via collar incision. ± lateral neck extension if lateral nodes involved.
operative_templates.position: Supine with neck extended, shoulder roll, head ring
operative_templates.incision: Collar (Kocher) incision 2-3cm above sternal notch, extended to both sternomastoids
steps: Collar incision through skin, platysma, raise subplatysmal flaps
Divide linea alba cervicalis, retract strap muscles laterally
Identify and expose both thyroid lobes
Start on one lobe: identify RLN in tracheoesophageal groove
Identify and preserve parathyroid glands (superior and inferior) with vascular pedicles
Ligate superior pole vessels close to thyroid capsule
Dissect RLN along its entire course to cricothyroid membrane
Ligate inferior thyroid artery branches on capsule
Resect thyroid lobe with Berry's ligament dissection off trachea
Central neck dissection: remove all lymphofatty tissue from hyoid bone superiorly to innominate artery inferiorly, and from carotid sheath laterally to midline
Repeat total lobectomy on contralateral side
keyLandmarks: RLN — in tracheoesophageal groove, posterior to inferior thyroid artery branches, enters larynx at cricothyroid
Parathyroids — superior at cricoid level, inferior near lower pole
Superior laryngeal nerve (external branch) — near superior pole vessels
Central compartment boundaries: hyoid (superior), carotid sheaths (lateral), innominate (inferior)

dischargeCriteria: Airway patent — no stridor or respiratory distress
Calcium stable (corrected >2.0 mmol/L) without IV requirement
Voice acceptable — mild hoarseness acceptable but no aspiration
Wound clean, drain removed or minimal output
Oral calcium and/or calcitriol prescribed if needed
Levothyroxine started (if total thyroidectomy) with dose education
Histology and MDT plan discussed with patient
Follow-up for Tg, calcium, TSH in 6-8 weeks scheduled

patient_education.explanation: Thyroid cancer is an abnormal growth of cells in the thyroid gland in your neck. Most types (papillary and follicular) have excellent outcomes when treated with surgery. Treatment involves removing all or part of the thyroid and any affected lymph nodes. You may need radioactive iodine therapy after surgery depending on your specific cancer type and stage. Regular follow-up with blood tests (thyroglobulin) and scans is essential.
warningSigns: After surgery: sudden neck swelling, difficulty breathing — return immediately
Tingling or numbness around the lips or fingers
Fever with wound redness or discharge
activity: Gentle neck exercises starting 1 week after surgery to prevent scar contracture
Return to work: 2-3 weeks for office jobs, 4-6 weeks for manual work
diet: After total thyroidectomy: no dietary restrictions needed
If you need radioactive iodine treatment: you will be given a low-iodine diet for 2 weeks before treatment (avoid iodized salt, seafood, dairy, eggs, red food coloring)
medication: You must take Levothyroxine daily for life after total thyroidectomy
Never miss a dose — take same time each day, 30 minutes before food
If you develop tingling/numbness: you may need calcium and Vitamin D supplements
If you need RAI: you will take rhTSH injections or stop thyroxine temporarily
followUp: Regular follow-up is essential for thyroid cancer surveillance
Blood tests: TSH, thyroglobulin, thyroglobulin antibodies every 6-12 months
Neck ultrasound every 6-12 months for first 2-3 years, then annually
If thyroglobulin rises: further imaging (RAI scan, CT, PET/CT)
Annual clinical neck exam for life

before_discharge: Follow-up schedule: 1 week for histology, 6 weeks for lab

after_discharge.action: Wound check, histology results discussion, MDT outcome
TSH, free T4, thyroglobulin, Tg antibodies, calcium. Adjust Levothyroxine dose.
Neck ultrasound — assess thyroid bed and lymph nodes
Tg, TSH, neck US. If RAI given: post-treatment whole body scan.
Annual surveillance: Tg, TSH, neck US. Continued for life.
Genetic counseling for MTC (RET testing) and family screening

when_to_return: Sudden neck swelling or difficulty breathing
Tingling or numbness not controlled by calcium
Voice hoarseness that is new or worsening
Fever with wound redness or discharge
New lump in the neck detected on self-exam

pathophysiologyDetail: Papillary thyroid carcinoma (PTC) arises from follicular epithelial cells; the BRAF V600E mutation (present in 40-60% of PTC) constitutively activates the MAPK pathway (RAF→MEK→ERK), driving proliferation, dedifferentiation, and lymph node metastasis

BRAF V600E-positive PTC is associated with taller cell morphology, higher T stage, more nodal metastases, higher recurrence, and RAI-refractoriness — it is the most important prognostic molecular marker in PTC
Follicular thyroid carcinoma (FTC) is driven by RAS mutations (40-50%) and PAX8/PPARγ rearrangements (30-40%); unlike PTC, FTC spreads hematogenously (bone, lung, brain) rather than lymphatically

The capsular and vascular invasion distinguish FTC from follicular adenoma: minimally invasive FTC has limited capsular invasion, while widely invasive FTC shows extensive capsular penetration and vascular involvement

Medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) arises from parafollicular C-cells (neural crest origin) that produce calcitonin; 25% are hereditary (RET germline mutation in MEN2A/MEN2B) and 75% are sporadic (RET somatic mutation)

C-cell hyperplasia is the precursor of hereditary MTC: RET activation causes progressive C-cell proliferation → microscopic MTC → macroscopic MTC → metastatic spread — this justifies prophylactic thyroidectomy in RET carriers

Anaplastic thyroid carcinoma (ATC) represents dedifferentiation of pre-existing differentiated thyroid cancer (PTC/FTC) through accumulation of additional mutations (TP53 loss, TERT promoter, CTNNB1/β-catenin) — it is one of the most aggressive human malignancies

The sodium-iodide symporter (NIS) on follicular cells mediates radioactive iodine uptake — loss of NIS expression (via BRAF/MAPK-induced silencing) makes RAI-refractory disease the main challenge in advanced thyroid cancer

Lymphatic spread of PTC follows a predictable pattern: tumor → perithyroidal lymphatics → central compartment (level VI) → lateral neck levels II-V. Skip metastases (lateral without central) occur in 10-20%

Thyroglobulin (Tg) is produced only by thyroid follicular cells (benign and malignant) — after total thyroidectomy + RAI ablation, Tg should be undetectable; a detectable or rising Tg is the earliest marker of recurrence

In Kenya, late presentation means larger tumors (often T3-T4), higher rates of extrathyroidal extension, and more extensive nodal involvement at diagnosis. RAI availability is limited, making complete initial surgery even more critical

The tumor microenvironment in PTC includes tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) and regulatory T-cells (Tregs) that promote immune evasion; PD-L1 expression on PTC cells is targetable by checkpoint inhibitors in advanced RAI-refractory disease

landmarkTrials.trial: Hay ID, et al. Papillary thyroid microcarcinoma — a 50-year Mayo Clinic experience
landmarkTrials.population: 900 patients with papillary microcarcinoma (<1cm) followed for up to 50 years
landmarkTrials.finding: Overall 20-year recurrence rate was 6% (0.3%/year). Tumor-related mortality was 0.3%. Lobectomy alone was as effective as total thyroidectomy for unifocal microPTC
landmarkTrials.impact: Established that most microPTC can be managed with lobectomy alone or even active surveillance, avoiding overtreatment

landmarkTrials.trial: Mazzaferri EL, et al. Long-term impact of initial surgical and medical therapy on PTC outcomes — Ohio State series
landmarkTrials.population: 1,355 patients with PTC and FTC followed for mean 15.7 years
landmarkTrials.finding: Total thyroidectomy reduced recurrence vs lobectomy (18% vs 32%). RAI reduced recurrence in patients with nodal mets (16% vs 38%). TSH suppression (<0.1 mU/L) improved survival in high-risk patients
landmarkTrials.impact: Established the three pillars of DTC management: total thyroidectomy, RAI ablation for high-risk patients, and TSH suppression
landmarkTrials.reference: Mazzaferri EL, et al. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 1994;78(4):771-778

landmarkTrials.trial: Schlumberger M, et al. Lenvatinib vs placebo in RAI-refractory thyroid cancer (SELECT)
landmarkTrials.population: 392 patients with RAI-refractory DTC randomized to Lenvatinib vs placebo
landmarkTrials.finding: Lenvatinib improved progression-free survival from 3.6 to 18.3 months (HR 0.21). Response rate 64.8%. No overall survival benefit at interim analysis
landmarkTrials.impact: Established Lenvatinib as first-line TKI for RAI-refractory metastatic DTC
landmarkTrials.reference: Schlumberger M, et al. N Engl J Med. 2015;372(7):621-630

landmarkTrials.trial: Wells SA Jr, et al. Vandetanib in advanced MTC (ZETA Trial)
landmarkTrials.population: 331 patients with advanced MTC randomized to Vandetanib vs placebo
landmarkTrials.finding: Vandetanib improved PFS from 19.3 to 30.5 months (HR 0.46). Objective response rate 45%. RET mutation carriers had greater benefit
landmarkTrials.impact: Established Vandetanib as first-line targeted therapy for advanced/metastatic MTC
landmarkTrials.reference: Wells SA Jr, et al. J Clin Oncol. 2012;30(2):134-141

landmarkTrials.trial: Dralle H, et al. Prophylactic thyroidectomy in MEN2 children — German MEN2 registry
landmarkTrials.population: 75 children from MEN2 families who underwent prophylactic thyroidectomy based on RET mutation and calcitonin levels
landmarkTrials.finding: Prophylactic thyroidectomy before age 6 (MEN2B) or age 10 (MEN2A) completely prevented MTC. Children with normal stimulated calcitonin had 100% cure rate
landmarkTrials.impact: Established that prophylactic thyroidectomy based on RET genotype is curative and the standard of care for MEN2 carriers

landmarkTrials.trial: Ain KB, et al. Dabrafenib + Trametinib in BRAF V600E-positive ATC (ROAR Basket Trial)
landmarkTrials.population: 36 patients with BRAF V600E-mutant anaplastic thyroid carcinoma
landmarkTrials.finding: Overall response rate 69%, complete response 18%. Median PFS 13.4 months, OS 18.7 months — dramatically better than historical controls (median OS 3-6 months)
landmarkTrials.impact: Established dabrafenib + trametinib as first-line therapy for BRAF-mutant ATC, transforming a universally fatal disease into a treatable one for mutation-positive patients
landmarkTrials.reference: Subbiah V, et al. J Clin Oncol. 2019;37(15_suppl):6001

evidenceTable.recommendation: Thyroid lobectomy alone is sufficient for unifocal, intrathyroidal PTC <4cm without nodal metastases or aggressive histology
Total thyroidectomy is indicated for PTC >4cm, bilateral disease, extrathyroidal extension, nodal metastases, or aggressive subtypes
evidenceTable.reference: ATA Guidelines; Mazzaferri EL, J Clin Endocrinol Metab 1994

evidenceTable.recommendation: Radioactive iodine ablation is recommended for high-risk and selected intermediate-risk DTC after total thyroidectomy
TSH suppression targets: low risk 0.5-2.0 mU/L, intermediate risk 0.1-0.5 mU/L, high risk <0.1 mU/L
Serum thyroglobulin (with Tg antibodies) and neck ultrasound are the mainstays of DTC surveillance
Lenvatinib is first-line therapy for RAI-refractory metastatic DTC
evidenceTable.reference: SELECT Trial, Schlumberger M, N Engl J Med 2015

evidenceTable.recommendation: Vandetanib or Cabozantinib are effective for advanced/metastatic MTC
evidenceTable.reference: ZETA Trial, Wells SA, J Clin Oncol 2012

evidenceTable.recommendation: Dabrafenib + Trametinib is first-line therapy for BRAF V600E-mutant anaplastic thyroid carcinoma
evidenceTable.reference: ROAR Basket Trial, Subbiah V, J Clin Oncol 2019

evidenceTable.recommendation: Prophylactic total thyroidectomy is recommended for RET mutation carriers based on codon-specific risk (MEN2B <age 1, MEN2A <age 5-10)
evidenceTable.reference: Dralle H, Surgery 2004; ATA MTC Guidelines

evidenceTable.recommendation: Central neck dissection (level VI) should be performed for clinically involved nodes or high-risk PTC; routine prophylactic central neck dissection is controversial
evidenceTable.reference: ATA Guidelines; Dralle H, World J Surg 2004

evidenceTable.recommendation: All patients with MTC should undergo RET germline genetic testing and pre-operative screening for pheochromocytoma (MEN2)

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: Thyroid nodule(s) identified on clinical exam or imaging → Thyroid Ultrasound with TIRADS scoring → TIRADS 5 (>1cm): FNA. TIRADS 4 (>1.5cm): FNA. TIRADS 3 (>2cm): FNA. TIRADS 1-2: benign — no FNA. → FNA with Bethesda classification → Bethesda V/VI (suspicious/malignant): surgery. Bethesda IV (follicular): diagnostic lobectomy. Bethesda III: repeat FNA or molecular testing. Bethesda II: benign — observe. → Pre-op workup: TSH, free T4. Calcitonin (screen for MTC). Neck US for lymph node survey. CT neck if T3-T4 or nodal disease. Calcium and PTH baseline. Vocal cord assessment. → If MTC suspected: RET genetic testing, MEN2 workup (pheo screening before surgery). → Surgery decision: T1-T2, unifocal, <4cm, NO, intraglandular → lobectomy. >4cm, bilateral, T3-T4, N1, aggressive subtype, MTC → total thyroidectomy + central neck dissection. Lateral neck nodes positive → lateral neck dissection (levels II-V). → If total thyroidectomy: start Levothyroxine 1.6mcg/kg post-op. → Histology: ATA risk stratification (low/intermediate/high). MDT discussion. → RAI decision: high risk → RAI ablation. Intermediate → individualize (consider age, tumor size, nodal burden, BRAF status). Low risk → no RAI. → Post-RAI: whole body scan at 5-7 days. TSH suppression. → Surveillance: Tg + TgAb + neck US every 6-12 months. TSH annual. If rising Tg: CT neck/chest, RAI scan, FDG-PET/CT.

managementPearls.context: Active surveillance for microPTC is an option
managementPearls.pearl: In Japan and increasingly in the US, active surveillance for microPTC (<1cm, no nodal mets, no aggressive features) is offered instead of immediate surgery. The risk of progression at 10 years is <5%, and delayed surgery after progression does not affect survival. Discuss this option with patients — many will choose surgery for peace of mind, but some will accept observation.
managementPearls.evidence: Ito Y, et al. Active surveillance of microPTC. World J Surg 2010

managementPearls.context: Incidental PTC on histology — what to do
managementPearls.pearl: When lobectomy for a benign indication returns with incidental PTC: if microPTC (<1cm) — no further surgery. If >1cm but low-risk (unifocal, intraglandular, no aggressive features) — the existing lobectomy is sufficient with 98% 20-year survival. Completion thyroidectomy only if: >4cm, multifocal, ETE, nodal mets, or high-risk subtype.

managementPearls.context: Central neck dissection — when and how
managementPearls.pearl: Therapeutic central neck dissection (cN1) is mandatory. Prophylactic central neck dissection (cN0) is controversial. My approach: do it for T3-T4 tumors, lateral neck mets, and when it will change RAI dosing. But be aware: prophylactic CND doubles the rate of permanent hypoparathyroidism (from 2% to 4%) without proven survival benefit. Discuss the trade-off with the patient.
managementPearls.evidence: ATA Guidelines; Dralle H, World J Surg 2004

managementPearls.context: Tg doubling time is the best surveillance tool
managementPearls.pearl: A detectable Tg after total thyroidectomy + RAI means persistent/recurrent disease. But the real power is Tg DOUBLING TIME. Tg DT <1 year is aggressive (consider PET/CT, TKI therapy). Tg DT >3 years is indolent (continue monitoring). Tg DT is a better predictor of progression than the absolute Tg level. Get 3 values 6-12 months apart for calculation.
managementPearls.evidence: Giovanella L, et al. Tg doubling time in DTC surveillance. J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2022

managementPearls.context: MTC — calcitonin is the tumor marker
managementPearls.pearl: Calcitonin half-life is 5 minutes. After total thyroidectomy, it should drop to undetectable within 12 hours. Persistent elevation means residual disease. A calcitonin level >100 pg/mL correlates with MTC, >500 pg/mL with lateral neck nodes, >2,000 pg/mL with distant metastases. Calcitonin doubling time predicts progression — <6 months is aggressive.

managementPearls.context: ATC — do not operate unless R0 is achievable
managementPearls.pearl: Anaplastic thyroid carcinoma is not a surgical disease unless the tumor is completely resectable (R0) with negative margins. Most patients present with unresectable disease invading trachea, esophagus, or great vessels. For these patients: biopsy to confirm BRAF V600E status → dabrafenib + trametinib if positive → then reassess for surgery. Tracheostomy for airway obstruction. MDT discussion is mandatory.
managementPearls.evidence: ROAR Basket Trial; ATA ATC Guidelines

operativeNuances.procedure: Total Thyroidectomy with Central Neck Dissection
operativeNuances.pitfall: The preserved parathyroid gland is inadvertently removed in the central neck specimen
operativeNuances.expertTip: Before sending the central neck dissection specimen to pathology, examine the tissue on the back table with loupe magnification. Identify any parathyroid glands that may have been included. If found: gently dissect them free, mince into 1mm fragments, and autotransplant into the sternocleidomastoid muscle. Label the graft site with a permanent suture or clip.

operativeNuances.procedure: Total Thyroidectomy with Central Neck Dissection
operativeNuances.pitfall: Incomplete resection of Berry's ligament leaves behind a small tongue of thyroid tissue (tubercle of Zuckerkandl) adherent to the RLN
operativeNuances.expertTip: The tubercle of Zuckerkandl is a posterolateral projection of thyroid tissue that frequently wraps around the RLN (present in 60-80% of patients). Carefully dissect the RLN through the tubercle: use micro-scissors, stay on the nerve, gently separate the tubercle from the nerve. If adherent, leave a 1mm remnant on the nerve rather than risk RLN injury.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Injury to the spinal accessory nerve (SAN) during Level II dissection, causing shoulder drop
operativeNuances.expertTip: The SAN runs from the posterior border of the sternocleidomastoid (Erb's point, 6cm above the clavicle at the posterior SCM border) diagonally across the posterior triangle into the trapezius. Use a nerve stimulator to locate the SAN early. Trace it from Erb's point proximally to the skull base and distally into the trapezius. Do NOT divide any structure that looks like a nerve in Level IIB without stimulation.

operativeNuances.pitfall: The RLN is encased in dense scar tissue from the previous surgery, making identification extremely hazardous
operativeNuances.expertTip: Re-operative thyroid surgery has 5-10x higher RLN injury rates. Approach from the LATERAL aspect: retract the strap muscles laterally, dissect on the anterior surface of the prevertebral fascia, identify the RLN where it enters the larynx (cricothyroid joint) — the nerve is most consistently found here and is often spared from scar. Then dissect the nerve retrograde. Use a nerve stimulator on every structure before dividing it.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Anastomotic dehiscence after tracheal resection — the most feared complication with high mortality
operativeNuances.expertTip: Key principles: (1) Resect no more than 4-5cm of trachea (6 rings) to allow primary anastomosis without tension. (2) Protect the anastomosis with a strap muscle or thymus flap. (3) Keep the chin-to-chest distance fixed with a chin stitch (keeping the neck flexed) for 7 days post-op. (4) Extubate awake in the OR to assess the anastomosis. (5) Avoid emergent reintubation d/t risk of anastomotic disruption. A tracheostomy placed through the anastomosis will cause stenosis.

prognosis.mortality: Papillary thyroid cancer: 5-year OS 98% (overall), >99% for low-risk, 85-95% for high-risk. Follicular: 5-year OS 95% (overall), 85% for widely invasive. Medullary: 5-year OS 80-90% (Stage I), 40-60% (Stage IV). Anaplastic: median OS 3-6 months historically, 14-19 months with targeted therapy (BRAF inhibitors). In Kenya, differentiated thyroid cancer has 10-15% lower 5-year survival due to late presentation and limited access to RAI.
prognosis.morbidity: Permanent hypoparathyroidism: 2-3% after total thyroidectomy alone, 5-10% with central neck dissection, 15-20% with bilateral lateral neck dissection. Permanent RLN injury: 1-2% first surgery, 5-10% re-operative. Transient RLN: 5%. Neck hematoma: 0.5-1% (can be fatal). Seroma: 5%. Hypothyroidism (after total): 100% — managed with Levothyroxine. RAI side effects: sialadenitis (30%), taste changes (10%), dry mouth (5%), bone marrow suppression (2%, transient).
prognosis.recurrence: Low-risk PTC: 2-5% recurrence at 10 years. Intermediate risk: 10-20%. High-risk: 30-40%. MTC: 20-30% recurrence at 10 years (higher for MEN2B and late-stage). FTC: 15-25% recurrence (mostly distant). ATC: 70-90% local recurrence within 6 months. Tg elevation as first sign of recurrence — 80% detectable before clinical or imaging evidence.
prognosis.survival: DTC 20-year disease-specific survival: low-risk 99%, intermediate 95%, high-risk 85%. Thyroglobulin doubling time is the strongest predictor: <1 year = high risk of progression, >3 years = very low risk. BRAF V600E confers 2-3x recurrence risk in PTC. Age >45 at diagnosis is an independent risk factor. RET mutation carriers: 100% develop MTC without prophylactic thyroidectomy; 100% cure with timely prophylactic surgery.
prognosis.reference: Hay ID, Surgery 2008; Mazzaferri EL, J Clin Endocrinol Metab 1994; ATA Guidelines; SELECT Trial; ROAR Basket Trial
